stem: A 31-year-old woman presents to her GP at 6 weeks postpartum for routine review. She is exclusively breastfeeding her healthy term infant 8 times in 24 hours including overnight. She wants effective long-term contraception because the family is complete. Her BMI is 24 kg/m^2, BP is 116/72 mmHg, and there is no personal or family history of VTE or breast cancer. She had an uncomplicated vaginal birth and would prefer not to have surgery for sterilisation at this time.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 70, RR 14, BP 116/72 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Well-healed perineum, uterus involuted, no abnormal discharge, BMI 24 kg/m^2

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate contraceptive option?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill starting today as effective contraception during breastfeeding
options.rationale: Combined oestrogen-containing methods are avoided in the first 6 weeks postpartum due to VTE risk, and can reduce breast milk supply; they are not first-line in lactating women. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception)

options.text: Insertion of a levonorgestrel intrauterine device at this visit with safety-net advice
options.rationale: LNG-IUD is a first-line long-acting reversible contraceptive that is safe in breastfeeding, can be inserted from 4 weeks postpartum, and is more than 99% effective. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception)

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method as primary contraception until the infant is 12 months of age
options.rationale: LAM is reliable only when the woman is fully breastfeeding, amenorrhoeic and less than 6 months postpartum; she has reached 6 weeks but a long-acting method better suits her family-complete goal. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception)

options.text: Vaginal contraceptive ring inserted today and changed every 3 weeks for ongoing protection
options.rationale: The combined hormonal ring contains oestrogen and is avoided during breastfeeding and within 6 weeks postpartum because of VTE and lactation impact. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception)

options.text: Condoms with safety-net of emergency contraception until laparoscopic tubal ligation is arranged
options.rationale: Condoms have higher typical-use failure than LARC and she wants effective long-term contraception without surgery; LNG-IUD better meets her stated preference. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception)

explanation.summary: Postnatal contraception at the 6-week check is an opportunity to provide effective long-acting reversible contraception. The LNG-IUD is safe during breastfeeding, can be inserted from 4 weeks postpartum, and is more than 99% effective. Oestrogen-containing methods are avoided within 6 weeks postpartum due to VTE risk and during breastfeeding due to lactation effects.
key_points: LNG-IUD is safe from 4 weeks postpartum and during breastfeeding
Oestrogen-containing combined methods are avoided in the first 6 weeks postpartum
Lactational amenorrhoea method requires full breastfeeding plus amenorrhoea under 6 months
explanation.pearls: Copper IUD and the etonogestrel implant are equally suitable LARC options in lactating women and follow the same insertion timing rules.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to her GP 3 weeks postpartum with 24 hours of fever, rigors and a painful left breast. She is exclusively breastfeeding her well infant. She has no penicillin allergy. On examination, the lateral upper quadrant of the left breast is red, warm and indurated with no fluctuance or skin breach. She is producing good volumes of milk on both sides. She has no axillary lymphadenopathy. The infant is feeding well.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.6 degrees Celsius, HR 100, RR 16, BP 118/74 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Left breast lateral upper quadrant erythema with induration, no fluctuance, no skin necrosis, no axillary nodes, nipples intact without cracks

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 7 days and cessation of breastfeeding from the affected breast
options.rationale: Amoxicillin lacks reliable Staphylococcus aureus cover and stopping breastfeeding worsens stasis; continued feeding is encouraged. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Mastitis)

options.text: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 to 7 days with continued breastfeeding from both breasts
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recommend oral flucloxacillin or dicloxacillin 500 mg QID for 5 to 7 days for lactational mastitis with constitutional symptoms, plus ongoing breastfeeding from the affected side to drain the duct. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Mastitis)

options.text: Symptomatic management with paracetamol and ibuprofen and review in 48 hours if not improving
options.rationale: Constitutional fever with breast erythema warrants antibiotic therapy at presentation; symptomatic management alone risks progression to abscess. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Mastitis)

options.text: Refer for urgent breast ultrasound to exclude abscess and defer antibiotics until imaging is reviewed
options.rationale: Without fluctuance or failure to improve, ultrasound is not required at presentation; antibiotics are started empirically and imaging reserved for failure or fluctuance. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Mastitis)

options.text: Oral cephalexin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days with cabergoline to suppress lactation temporarily
options.rationale: Suppressing lactation worsens stasis and prolongs mastitis; cabergoline has no role and 14 days is longer than required. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Mastitis)

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis with fever and a tender wedge of breast erythema is treated with oral flucloxacillin or dicloxacillin 500 mg QID for 5 to 7 days, with continued breastfeeding from the affected breast to maintain drainage. Symptomatic management alone is insufficient when constitutional symptoms are present. Ultrasound is reserved for failed antibiotic response or fluctuance suggesting abscess.
key_points: Flucloxacillin 500 mg QID for 5 to 7 days is the AU first-line for lactational mastitis
Continue breastfeeding from the affected side; do not suppress lactation
Ultrasound is for failure to respond at 48 hours or fluctuance suggesting abscess
explanation.pearls: If a deep breast abscess is found on ultrasound, image-guided needle aspiration is preferred over open incision and drainage to preserve future lactation.

stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman at 36+0 weeks is admitted to a tertiary obstetric unit with severe headache, epigastric pain and brisk reflexes. Her BP on admission is 168/108 mmHg confirmed on two readings 15 minutes apart. She has received IV labetalol and magnesium sulfate. The fetus is cephalic with a reassuring CTG and estimated fetal weight at the 40th centile. Her cervix is unfavourable (Bishop score 2). Investigations show evidence of severe disease.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 96, RR 18, BP 162/106 mmHg after labetalol, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes, no clonus, no visual disturbance after analgesia; epigastric tenderness; uterus non-tender; CTG with normal baseline and accelerations
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 86 x10^9/L, ALT 142 U/L, AST 156 U/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, urine protein-creatinine ratio 380 mg/mmol

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue magnesium and antihypertensive therapy as outpatient pending fetal movement diary monitoring
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia is never managed as outpatient; ongoing inpatient observation is required regardless of immediate delivery plan. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer antenatal corticosteroids and continue conservative management to 37+0 weeks
options.rationale: Antenatal steroids are not given beyond 35+6 weeks routinely; severe pre-eclampsia at 36+0 mandates delivery rather than further conservative management. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange elective caesarean section because the cervix is unfavourable for induction at term
options.rationale: Unfavourable cervix is not in itself an indication for caesarean section; induction with cervical ripening is appropriate unless other obstetric contraindications are present. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed with planned delivery during this admission with intrapartum magnesium continuation
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia at 36+0 with end-organ involvement (HELLP-spectrum thrombocytopenia and raised transaminases) is an indication for delivery regardless of cervical status; magnesium sulfate is continued through delivery and for 24 hours postpartum. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Transfer to a quaternary intensive care unit for plasma exchange before considering operative delivery
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is not part of pre-eclampsia or HELLP management; delivery is the definitive treatment. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia at or beyond 34 weeks with end-organ involvement is an indication for delivery during the index admission. Magnesium sulfate is continued through labour and for 24 hours postpartum to prevent seizures, and blood pressure is controlled with labetalol, nifedipine or hydralazine. Mode of delivery is decided on standard obstetric grounds; an unfavourable cervix is not alone an indication for caesarean.
key_points: Severe pre-eclampsia at 34 weeks or beyond mandates delivery
Magnesium sulfate is continued through delivery and 24 hours postpartum
Mode of delivery follows standard obstetric criteria; cervix score is not the driver
explanation.pearls: Platelet count under 100 x10^9/L combined with transaminitis and epigastric pain define HELLP; the diagnosis adds urgency but the management is still timely delivery with magnesium cover.

stem: A 28-year-old G3P2 woman at 32+0 weeks presents to a tertiary maternity unit by ambulance with sudden onset constant abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding 200 mL. The uterus is tense and tender between contractions. CTG shows a baseline of 160 with reduced variability and recurrent late decelerations. There is no placenta praevia on the routine 20-week ultrasound. Her BP is 102/64 mmHg after a 500 mL crystalloid bolus.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 122, RR 22, BP 102/64 mmHg, SpO2 98% in room air
data_table.Examination: Pale, anxious, tense tender uterus, palpable contractions every 2 minutes, cervix dilated 2 cm with old and fresh blood at os, fetal parts difficult to palpate
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, platelets 168 x10^9/L, fibrinogen 1.8 g/L, INR 1.4, group and screen sent; bedside ultrasound confirms cephalic presentation, posterior placenta with no praevia

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Augment labour with oxytocin and aim for vaginal delivery within the next 2 hours under monitoring
options.rationale: With pathological CTG and active abruption, delay for vaginal birth risks fetal demise; expedited delivery by caesarean is required unless birth is imminent. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Administer antenatal corticosteroids and tocolysis with nifedipine to delay delivery for 48 hours
options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in significant abruption with fetal distress and maternal compromise; delivery cannot be safely delayed for steroid effect. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Activate the obstetric emergency team and proceed to emergency caesarean section with massive transfusion protocol on standby
options.rationale: Suspected placental abruption with maternal compromise, fetal distress on CTG and coagulopathy is an indication for immediate delivery; the fastest safe route at 32 weeks with a 2 cm cervix is caesarean section. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Transfer to a quaternary fetal medicine unit for cordocentesis before deciding on delivery mode
options.rationale: Transfer for cordocentesis is not appropriate when the woman is unstable and the fetus is compromised; on-site delivery is needed. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Continue conservative management with intravenous fluids and serial CTG every 2 hours overnight
options.rationale: Conservative management is not appropriate with fetal distress, maternal coagulopathy and ongoing bleeding; delivery cannot be deferred. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Major placental abruption with fetal distress, maternal coagulopathy and active bleeding mandates immediate delivery. Caesarean section is the fastest safe route at 32 weeks with an unfavourable cervix. The massive transfusion protocol is activated for ongoing blood loss and coagulopathy; tocolysis and corticosteroids are not appropriate when delivery cannot be delayed.
key_points: Major abruption with fetal distress requires emergency delivery, usually caesarean
Tocolysis is contraindicated when active abruption causes fetal compromise
Activate MTP for coagulopathic obstetric haemorrhage; aim fibrinogen above 2 g/L
explanation.pearls: Concealed abruption can present with disproportionate maternal shock relative to visible blood loss; couvelaire uterus and DIC are recognised complications and reinforce the need for early delivery and product replacement.

stem: A 33-year-old woman presents with her partner to a gynaecology clinic after three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages. All losses were before 10 weeks with confirmed intrauterine pregnancies on ultrasound. She has regular cycles, BMI 24 kg/m^2, no smoking and no thrombotic history. The couple's karyotypes and a pelvic ultrasound are normal. There is no family history of recurrent miscarriage or VTE. She has not had previous investigations performed.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 72, RR 14, BP 116/72 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Normal external genitalia and bimanual examination, no abdominal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic ultrasound with 3D imaging shows normal uterine cavity; thyroid function normal; HbA1c 5.2%; karyotypes of both partners normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next test?

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia panel including factor V Leiden and prothrombin gene variant testing
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended in recurrent first-trimester miscarriage; the evidence base does not support routine investigation or anticoagulation for this group. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibody screen including lupus anticoagulant and anticardiolipin antibodies on two occasions
options.rationale: Antiphospholipid syndrome is a treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage; Australian guidelines recommend lupus anticoagulant and anti-cardiolipin (and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein 1) tested 12 weeks apart in women with three first-trimester losses. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Hysteroscopy to assess the uterine cavity for septum or polyps missed by ultrasound imaging
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is reserved for abnormal ultrasound or saline sonohysterography findings; a normal 3D ultrasound makes it unlikely to add diagnostic information. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Natural killer cell phenotyping and uterine immunohistochemistry to assess immune cause of loss
options.rationale: NK cell testing and immunological treatments are not supported by Australian guidelines for recurrent miscarriage; they are not part of routine investigation. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Anti-Mullerian hormone level and antral follicle count to assess ovarian reserve before reassurance
options.rationale: Ovarian reserve testing does not investigate recurrent miscarriage; AMH does not predict miscarriage rates and is not part of the work-up. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

explanation.summary: Recurrent first-trimester miscarriage in Australia is investigated with parental karyotyping, pelvic imaging and antiphospholipid antibody screen. Antiphospholipid syndrome is the most common treatable cause and is diagnosed when lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin or anti-beta-2-glycoprotein 1 are persistently abnormal on two samples 12 weeks apart. Inherited thrombophilia panels, NK cell tests and ovarian reserve testing are not recommended.
key_points: APS is the major treatable cause and requires positive tests on two occasions 12 weeks apart
Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended in first-trimester recurrent loss
NK cell testing and immune therapies are not supported by Australian guidelines
explanation.pearls: Confirmed APS in subsequent pregnancy is treated with low-dose aspirin and prophylactic enoxaparin from a positive pregnancy test until 6 weeks postpartum, which roughly doubles live birth rates.
